text:
Create a Server Action that creates a new post. The posts list uses cache tags for caching.

After creating the post, invalidate the "posts" cache tag. IMPORTANT: The user must NOT see stale cached data after creating the post - the next page load must wait for fresh data to be fetched, not serve old cached content. Use the appropriate Next.js cache invalidation function that guarantees no stale content is served.

I’m locating the posts data flow and existing cache-tag usage first, then I’ll add the create-post Server Action and wire cache invalidation so the next load blocks on fresh data instead of serving stale results.

text: The repo is minimal and doesn’t have the posts implementation yet. I’m reading the app files and package config now so I can add the smallest complete path for create, list, and tag-based invalidation correctly.
I found an empty app, so I’m building the full minimal example and verifying the exact invalidation API against current Next.js docs before I edit files.

I have the API choice pinned down: this needs `updateTag('posts')`, not `revalidateTag`, because `revalidateTag` can serve stale content while background refresh runs. I’m patching in a cached posts reader, file-backed storage, and a form-backed Server Action now.
The create flow is in place. I’m running a build now to catch any App Router or cache-component issues and fix them before I hand this over.
The build is still running. I’m waiting on the final Next.js compile result, then I’ll make any compatibility fixes if the cache APIs need adjustment in this canary version.

text:
Implemented a create-post Server Action and wired cache-tag invalidation so the next load waits for fresh data.

The key part is [app/actions.ts](/home/user/workspace/app/actions.ts:1): after persisting the new post, it calls `updateTag('posts')`. That is the correct API for your requirement because it immediately expires the tagged cache entry; the next request blocks for fresh data instead of serving stale cached content. I did not use `revalidateTag` for that reason.

The posts list is now read through a cache-tagged cached function in [lib/posts.ts](/home/user/workspace/lib/posts.ts:27), and the page renders a form that submits to the Server Action in [app/page.tsx](/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx:1). I also enabled Cache Components in [next.config.ts](/home/user/workspace/next.config.ts:1) so `use cache`/`cacheTag` work as intended.

Verified with `npm run build`; it passes.
